content.text: Data collection is the first and most consequential step in preparing a training dataset for LLM fine-tuning or continued pretraining. The quality, diversity, and domain coverage of collected data determines the ceiling of model capability — no amount of architectural innovation or compute can compensate for a poorly collected or biased dataset. For production fine-tuning, data collection encompasses web crawling, dataset curation, synthetic generation, human annotation, and the aggregation of proprietary sources into a format ready for quality filtering and deduplication.
LLM training datasets draw from five primary source categories, each with distinct collection methods, quality profiles, and licensing considerations:

attrs.alt: LLM data collection pipeline: five source types (web crawls, curated datasets, proprietary data, synthetic, human-labeled) flow through quality filtering, deduplication/tokenization, and domain mixing stages.

content.text: Web crawls are the largest source of raw text for pretraining. Common Crawl provides petabyte-scale snapshots of the web, updated monthly, as WARC (Web ARChive) files containing HTTP responses with raw HTML. Extracting clean text from raw HTML requires a text extraction pipeline:

# Extract clean text from Common Crawl WARC files using trafilatura
import trafilatura
import warc   # pip install warc3-wet-clueweb09

def extract_text_from_warc(warc_path: str, min_chars: int = 200):
    """Extract clean main-content text from a WARC file."""
    results = []
    with warc.open(warc_path) as warc_file:
        for record in warc_file:
            if record['WARC-Type'] != 'response':
                continue
            url = record['WARC-Target-URI']
            html = record.payload.read()
            # trafilatura: extracts main content, removes boilerplate
            text = trafilatura.extract(
                html,
                include_comments=False,
                include_tables=True,
                no_fallback=False,    # use fallback extractor if primary fails
                favor_precision=True, # prioritise quality over recall
            )
            if text and len(text) >= min_chars:
                results.append({"url": url, "text": text})
    return results

# Scale: Common Crawl monthly snapshot ≈ 200-300 TB WARC; yields ~20-30 TB clean text
# After quality filtering: FineWeb (2024) extracted 15T tokens from 95 Common Crawl dumps

Domain-specific crawls target high-quality subsets of the web. For a legal AI, you would crawl court databases (CourtListener, PACER), law reviews, and bar association publications. For a biomedical model, PubMed full-text and clinical trial registries. Targeted crawls yield much higher precision than sampling Common Crawl and filtering.
Several large-scale datasets have been assembled from diverse sources and released publicly for research and commercial use:
CC, C4, Reddit, books, code, Wikipedia

When using public datasets, verify that your deployment use case is compatible with the dataset license. FineWeb and Dolma use the Open Data Commons Attribution License (ODC-By), which requires attribution but allows commercial use. Some datasets (The Pile v1) have been removed from HuggingFace Hub due to copyright disputes — use updated versions or check provenance carefully.
For domain-specific fine-tuning, proprietary data is often the highest-signal source. This includes:
Internal wikis, product documentation, support tickets, email archives, and policy manuals. Requires PII scrubbing and access control — employees should consent to training use.
SQL queries and results, product metadata, transaction records in text form. Must be anonymized; never train on raw financial or health records without IRB approval.
Chat transcripts, support ticket threads, call transcripts (post-ASR). High signal for conversational fine-tuning; strict privacy requirements apply.
Textbooks, research papers, technical standards (if licensed). For medical: clinical practice guidelines, pharmacology references, case report archives.

# PII scrubbing pipeline using presidio (Microsoft open-source)
from presidio_analyzer import AnalyzerEngine
from presidio_anonymizer import AnonymizerEngine
from presidio_anonymizer.entities import OperatorConfig

analyzer = AnalyzerEngine()
anonymizer = AnonymizerEngine()

def scrub_pii(text: str, entities_to_scrub: list[str] | None = None) -> str:
    """
    Detect and replace PII in text.
    Default scrubs: PERSON, EMAIL_ADDRESS, PHONE_NUMBER, CREDIT_CARD, SSN,
                    MEDICAL_LICENSE, IP_ADDRESS, US_PASSPORT, DATE_TIME
    """
    if entities_to_scrub is None:
        entities_to_scrub = [
            "PERSON", "EMAIL_ADDRESS", "PHONE_NUMBER", "CREDIT_CARD",
            "US_SSN", "IP_ADDRESS", "URL", "LOCATION",
        ]

    results = analyzer.analyze(
        text=text,
        entities=entities_to_scrub,
        language="en",
    )

    anonymized = anonymizer.anonymize(
        text=text,
        analyzer_results=results,
        operators={
            "PERSON": OperatorConfig("replace", {"new_value": "<PERSON>"}),
            "EMAIL_ADDRESS": OperatorConfig("replace", {"new_value": "<EMAIL>"}),
            "PHONE_NUMBER": OperatorConfig("replace", {"new_value": "<PHONE>"}),
            "CREDIT_CARD": OperatorConfig("replace", {"new_value": "<CC_NUMBER>"}),
            "US_SSN": OperatorConfig("replace", {"new_value": "<SSN>"}),
        }
    )
    return anonymized.text

# Apply at scale with multiprocessing:
from multiprocessing import Pool
with Pool(processes=16) as pool:
    cleaned_texts = pool.map(scrub_pii, raw_texts)

LLM-generated synthetic data has become a primary training signal for instruction-following, reasoning, and domain knowledge — particularly when high-quality human-labeled data is scarce. The synthetic data pipeline uses a capable frontier model (GPT-4, Claude 3, Llama-3 70B) as a teacher to generate diverse, high-quality examples:

import openai
import json
from typing import Generator

def generate_instruction_pairs(
    topics: list[str],
    n_per_topic: int = 50,
    model: str = "gpt-4o",
) -> Generator[dict, None, None]:
    """
    Generate instruction-response pairs for fine-tuning using a frontier model.
    Each call to the API costs ~$0.01-0.05 per pair for gpt-4o.
    """
    client = openai.OpenAI()

    SYSTEM = """You are a helpful assistant generating diverse training examples.
For each topic, generate a realistic user question and a high-quality, accurate answer.
Format: {"instruction": "...", "response": "..."}
The instruction should be phrased as a natural user request.
The response should be accurate, complete, and appropriately detailed.
"""

    for topic in topics:
        prompt = f"""Generate {n_per_topic} diverse instruction-response pairs about: {topic}
Each pair should cover a different aspect or difficulty level.
Output as a JSON array of objects with 'instruction' and 'response' keys."""

        response = client.chat.completions.create(
            model=model,
            messages=[
                {"role": "system", "content": SYSTEM},
                {"role": "user", "content": prompt},
            ],
            response_format={"type": "json_object"},
            temperature=0.9,
        )

        try:
            data = json.loads(response.choices[0].message.content)
            for pair in data.get("pairs", data.get("examples", [])):
                if "instruction" in pair and "response" in pair:
                    yield {
                        "instruction": pair["instruction"],
                        "response": pair["response"],
                        "topic": topic,
                        "source": "synthetic",
                    }
        except (json.JSONDecodeError, KeyError):
            continue   # skip malformed responses

# Cost estimate: 50 pairs/topic × 500 topics × $0.02/pair = ~$500 for 25,000 pairs
# Quality varies; always validate synthetic data with human review or LLM judge

Synthetic data quality depends critically on prompt design. Vague prompts produce generic, repetitive examples. Explicit requirements — 'include a code example', 'vary the difficulty from beginner to expert', 'include common misconceptions' — produce higher-diversity datasets that improve fine-tuning outcomes.
Human annotation is the highest-cost but highest-quality data source. It is essential for:
Annotators rank model responses on helpfulness, honesty, and harmlessness. Each annotation costs $1–10 depending on complexity; 50K–500K pairs are needed for RLHF.
Medical, legal, or scientific content that requires professional expertise to label accurately. Board-certified physicians for clinical data, licensed attorneys for legal text.
Red-teamers and domain experts craft inputs designed to elicit incorrect or unsafe responses, providing critical training signal for safety fine-tuning.

# Managing annotation tasks with Label Studio (self-hosted annotation platform)
# pip install label-studio

from label_studio_sdk import Client
from label_studio_sdk.data_manager import Filters, Column, Type, Operator

ls = Client(url="http://localhost:8080", api_key="your-api-key")

# Create annotation project for instruction-response preference pairs
project = ls.create_project(
    title="LLM Response Quality Annotation",
    label_config="""
<View>
  <Header value="Rate the quality of this AI response:" />
  <Text name="instruction" value="$instruction"/>
  <Text name="response_a" value="$response_a" header="Response A:"/>
  <Text name="response_b" value="$response_b" header="Response B:"/>
  <Choices name="preference" toName="response_a" choice="single">
    <Choice value="A is better"/>
    <Choice value="B is better"/>
    <Choice value="Both equal"/>
    <Choice value="Both poor"/>
  </Choices>
  <Textarea name="reason" toName="instruction" placeholder="Brief reason for your choice"/>
</View>
    """,
    description="Annotators rate which of two model responses is better.",
)

# Upload tasks (pairs of responses to compare)
tasks = [
    {
        "instruction": "Explain how attention works in transformers",
        "response_a": "...",   # model A output
        "response_b": "...",   # model B output
    }
    for _ in range(1000)
]
project.import_tasks(tasks)
print(f"Project ID: {project.id} — assign annotators in the Label Studio UI")
The right amount of training data depends on the fine-tuning objective and the model size. Empirical scaling laws provide rough guidelines:
Diversity of comparisons matters more than quantity

The Chinchilla scaling law (Hoffmann et al., 2022) showed that optimal token count for pretraining grows proportionally with parameter count. For fine-tuning, quality trumps scale once a minimum threshold is met. Alpaca achieved competitive instruction-following from 52K GPT-3.5-generated examples — far fewer than the millions of human-labeled examples previously assumed necessary.
Before moving from collection to quality filtering, verify:
License compatibility confirmed for all data sources — commercial use permitted for the intended deployment.
PII scrubbing applied to all human-authored content (names, emails, phone numbers, addresses, SSNs).
Sampling strategy documented — which sources, what proportions, and why.
Held-out evaluation set created from the same distribution before any filtering (prevents data contamination).
Provenance metadata retained — source URL, collection date, processing version — for auditing and deduplication.
Benchmark contamination check planned — will be executed in the filtering stage to remove examples overlapping test sets.
Legal review completed for proprietary internal data — employee consent, data retention policies, and third-party data agreements.

Data collection for LLM fine-tuning draws from five source types: web crawls (Common Crawl via trafilatura), curated public datasets (FineWeb, Dolma, RedPajama), proprietary internal data (with PII scrubbing via Presidio), synthetic LLM-generated examples (via GPT-4 or similar), and human-labeled annotation (preference pairs, expert labels). Volume requirements range from 500 examples for simple SFT to billions of tokens for domain-adaptive pretraining. The output of data collection feeds directly into quality filtering and deduplication — no model training should begin without a documented collection strategy, license audit, PII scrub, and held-out evaluation split.
